competency_label: Career Pathing — The Coordinator-to-PM Decision and the Development Conversation

dimensions.name: honest_self_assessment
dimensions.description: Does the learner engage the chapter's honest questions genuinely — do I want the accountability? the political exposure? to spend less time on artifacts and more on people? to be wrong publicly? — and reason from their own evidence (what has energised or drained them), rather than chasing the title or avoiding the question? A defensible 'yes', 'no', or 'not yet' are all fine; an un-examined answer is not.

anchors.1: No real self-assessment — wants the title because it's the next rung / a pay bump (title-chasing), or abdicates ('whatever the company wants').
anchors.3: Engages some of the honest questions but partially, or asserts a direction without grounding it in their own evidence.
anchors.5: Works the honest questions against their own experience (what's energised vs drained them) and reaches a reasoned, self-aware position — yes, no, or not-yet.

dimensions.name: role_change_understanding
dimensions.description: Does the learner understand that PM is a role CHANGE, not a bigger version of coordinator — a coordinator supports, a PM owns (accountable to the sponsor for the outcome) — and that some skills must be built new (sponsor management, strategic framing, accountability under pressure), so 'I do the coordinator job well' is necessary but not sufficient?

anchors.1: Treats PM as a promotion/reward for good coordination ('I've earned it'); assumes doing coordinator well makes them a good PM automatically.
anchors.3: Acknowledges PM is different but vaguely; doesn't name the specific new skills the coordinator role doesn't exercise.
anchors.5: Frames PM as a distinct role (support vs own), names specific skills to build new (sponsor management, accountability, strategic framing), and that coordinator excellence is necessary-not-sufficient.

dimensions.name: development_plan_concreteness
dimensions.description: Does the learner propose concrete, well-judged development steps that build the RIGHT (new) skills — ask for stretch responsibilities (a sponsor briefing, a small workshop, a vendor conversation), own something small end-to-end, find a mentor, observe the PM intentionally — sized appropriately, rather than a vague aspiration?

anchors.1: No development steps, or only the demand for a title/role with no plan to build toward it.
anchors.3: Some development intent but generic ('get more experience', 'do training') or not targeted at the new skills.
anchors.5: Specific, appropriately-sized stretch asks targeted at the skills the coordinator role doesn't exercise (own a small piece end-to-end, draft a sponsor briefing, a named mentor).

dimensions.name: agency_and_realism
dimensions.description: Does the learner own the decision with agency and realism — making a defensible choice (PM path, senior-IC/specialist, or not-yet) and accepting the real costs of whichever they choose — rather than abdicating the decision to others or assuming entitlement to the next rung?

anchors.1: Abdicates ('I'll do whatever they decide') OR assumes entitlement to the promotion; no ownership of the choice or its costs.
anchors.3: Takes a position but glosses the real costs/trade-offs of the path chosen, or hedges without committing.
anchors.5: Owns a defensible choice and is clear-eyed about its costs (e.g. accepts the accountability/political weight of PM, or the trade-offs of staying senior-IC); either path is valid.

dimensions.name: professional_framing
dimensions.description: Is the development conversation framed professionally with the PM — thoughtful, specific, and appropriately pitched (a request and a reflection, not a demand or an apology) — the kind of conversation a PM would respond to generously?

anchors.1: Entitled/demanding ('I want the PM role') OR apologetic/passive; not a professional development conversation.
anchors.3: Reasonable and professional but a little flat — the ask or the reflection is underdeveloped or mis-pitched.
anchors.5: Pitched as a thoughtful development conversation — honest reflection plus a specific, reasonable ask — that a PM would engage with seriously.
